title: Is It Too Late To Consider JPMorgan Chase (JPM) After Its Strong Share Price Run? - simplywall.st
content:
Find out why JPMorgan Chase's 36.2% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers. The Excess Returns model looks at how efficiently a company uses shareholders' equity, then estimates what that efficiency could be worth over time. Instead of focusing on cash flows, it compares the return generated on equity to the cost of that equity. For JPMorgan Chase, the model uses a Book Value of $126.99 per share and a Stable EPS of $24.22 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 13 analysts. The Average Return on Equity is 16.68%, while the Cost of Equity is $11.54 per share. The gap between what JPMorgan Chase is expected to earn and what it needs to earn for shareholders is captured in an Excess Return of $12.68 per share. The analysis also assumes a Stable Book Value of $145.16 per share, sourced from weighted future Book Value estimates from the same 13 analysts. Together, these inputs feed into an intrinsic value estimate of about $424.38 per share. This sits roughly 26.1% above the current price of around $313, indicating that the stock screens as undervalued under this approach. Our Excess Returns analysis suggests JPMorgan Chase is undervalued by 26.1%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 55 more high quality undervalued stocks. Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for JPMorgan Chase. For a profitable bank like JPMorgan Chase, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much investors are paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties into what the market is willing to pay for current profits, rather than balance sheet size or revenue alone. What counts as a “normal” P/E often reflects a mix of growth expectations and perceived risk. Higher expected earnings growth or a stronger balance sheet can support a higher multiple, while more cyclicality or risk usually calls for a lower one. JPMorgan Chase currently trades on a P/E of 15.11x, compared with an average of 13.78x for its peers and 11.90x for the Banks industry overall. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for JPMorgan Chase is higher, at 22.47x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what the P/E might be, given factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, risk profile, market cap and the characteristics of the banking industry. Because the Fair Ratio incorporates these company specific drivers, it can be more informative than a simple comparison with peer or industry averages. With a Fair Ratio of 22.47x versus the current 15.11x, JPMorgan Chase appears undervalued on this P/E based approach. P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 18 top founder-led companies. Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so meet Narratives, a simple way for you to attach a clear story about JPMorgan Chase to the numbers you care about, like your fair value, and your assumptions for future revenue, earnings and margins. A Narrative connects three things in one place: what you think is happening in the business, how that flows into a financial forecast, and what share price you see as fair value based on those assumptions. On Simply Wall St’s Community page, Narratives are available as an easy to use tool that many investors already rely on. They help you compare your own view of JPMorgan Chase with others, and quickly see how your Fair Value stacks up against the current Price when you are thinking about buying or selling. Because Narratives update automatically when new information like earnings releases or fresh news hits the stock, your fair value view can stay aligned with the latest data instead of becoming stale. For JPMorgan Chase, one investor might align with the more optimistic Narrative that uses a Fair Value of US$400, while another might lean toward the more cautious Narrative closer to US$283. By seeing both side by side, you can decide which story and valuation feel more realistic to you. For JPMorgan Chase, we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading JPMorgan Chase Narratives: Fair value in this bullish Narrative: US$337.75 per share. Implied undervaluation versus the last close of US$313.68: about 7.1%. Analyst revenue growth assumption used in this Narrative: about 7.6% a year. Fair value in this more cautious Narrative: about US$283.37 per share. Implied overvaluation versus the last close of US$313.68: about 10.7%. Revenue growth assumption used in this Narrative: about 7.2% a year. If you want to go beyond the previews and see how other investors are framing growth, risks, and fair value for JPMorgan Chase, it is worth reading the Narratives in full to test which story fits your own expectations. title: Concerns Beneath Wall Street's Boom: Trading and Investment Banking Drive JPMorgan (JPM.US) Q1 Results Beyond Expectations, Shadow of Iran War Looms Over Outlook - Moomoo
content: To create a seamless experience and for website quality and improvement purposes, we use cookie technologies for verification and quality purposes, to personalize the content presented, and to provide advertising that may be of specific interest to you. Learn our Privacy Policy here. 智通财经APP获悉，摩根大通(JPM.US)周二公布的第一季度业绩超出预期，主要得益于其固定收益和投资银行业务收入强于预期。LSEG数据显示，营收505.4亿美元，同比增长10%，高于预期的491.7亿美元。净利润增长13%至164.9亿美元，即每股5.94美元，高于预期的5.45美元。 近几个月来，随着美国劳动力市场韧性支撑工资增长并吸引更多借款人进入信贷市场，贷款需求有所回升。尽管利率仍然居高不下，但已从近期高位回落，这有助于提振贷款需求。 摩根大通第一季度净利息收入增长9%至254亿美元，超出预期。与此同时，该季度成本为269亿美元，高于预期。摩根大通在2月份表示，预计今年支出约为1050亿美元(不包括法律费用)，并在周二重申了这一数字。 尽管面临经济压力，消费者支出总体保持稳定，使坏账核销保持稳定，并增强了银行保持信贷流动的信心。包括摩根大通和美国银行(BAC.US)在内的大型贷款机构反映了美国经济的趋势，为了解美国经济提供了窗口。本季度，摩根大通美国消费者的财务状况似乎较为稳定。借记卡和信用卡总支出较2025年第一季度增长了9%。逾期90天以上的贷款率略微下降至1.15%，而去年同期为1.6%。 然而，近几个月来，美国经济的韧性支撑了消费支出和企业活动，但随着地缘政治紧张局势加剧，前景变得更加不明朗。值得注意的是，该银行下调了2026年全年净利息收入指引，净利息收入是银行盈利的关键驱动因素，从之前的1045亿美元下调至约1030亿美元。 自特朗普赢得2024年美国大选以来，华尔街交易部门一直处于火热状态。他的政策举措不断冲击着股票、利率和大宗商品市场，刺激了客户交易活动，也让银行从促成这些交易中获利颇丰。 而今年在第一季度，对人工智能对软件公司影响的担忧以及伊朗战争结果的不确定性，扰乱了全球金融市场，引发了多轮抛售，使得交易部门异常繁忙。市场波动通常会提振大型银行的交易业务，因为它会促使客户重新平衡投资组合、更积极地进行交易并对冲风险。 由于大宗商品、信贷、货币和新兴市场交易活动增加，摩根大通第一季度市场收入增长20%至116亿美元，是该行业绩的关键驱动因素，正如华尔街竞争对手高盛(GS.US)一样，后者周一公布的季度业绩也超出预期。该行的固定收益交易收入增长21%至70.8亿美元，比StreetAccount的预期高出约3.7亿美元。而股票业务收入飙升17%至45亿美元。 美国投资银行预计今年将迎来强劲增长，他们密切关注大型人工智能和航天公司的巨额上市，并寄希望于特朗普政府放松监管，从而推动并购交易的复苏。尽管市场波动导致对并购活动的预测较为谨慎，但银行高管表示，企业对并购交易表现出浓厚的兴趣。 摩根大通Q1投资银行业务费用收入增长28%至28.8亿美元，比预期高出约2.6亿美元，主要得益于并购咨询和股票承销费用的增加。其中，并购交易顾问费用表现尤为突出，飙升82%至12.7亿美元。股权承销费用也增幅超出预期，达到4.72亿美元，而债务承销费用则略低于预期，下降了7%。 根据Dealogic的数据，摩根大通的投资银行费用在第一季度同比增长28%，增幅位居同期全球银行之首。并购交易总额突破1万亿美元。 在本季度的主要交易中，摩根大通担任了科技巨头亚马逊370亿美元债券发行的簿记管理人，并担任AES宣布的334亿美元私有化交易的首席顾问。它也是软银旗下金融科技公司PayPay 3月份在美国8.8亿美元首次公开募股的主要承销商之一。 规模达1.8万亿美元的私募信贷行业一直是关注焦点，人们日益担忧赎回请求和人工智能的影响将对该行业造成压力。对银行而言，这意味着投资者对其向该行业放贷提出质疑。今年早些时候，摩根大通下调了部分贷款的价值，这些贷款是该行向私募信贷基金发放贷款的抵押品。 “从大局来看，私募信贷可能不会构成系统性风险，”戴蒙在本月初致股东的年度信中写道。“当信贷周期到来时(这种情况终将发生)，所有杠杆贷款的损失总体上都会高于预期，与当前环境相比。这是因为信贷标准几乎全面略有放松。” 从更广泛的信贷层面来看，摩根大通第一季度为潜在不良贷款拨备的资金增加了1.91亿美元，低于分析师的预期。具体而言，该行本季度减少了1.39亿美元的消费者贷款亏损准备金，但增加了3.27亿美元的企业贷款亏损准备金。 因此，另一个促成该行本季度业绩超出预期的因素是：其贷款损失准备金低于分析师预期。该行信贷损失准备金为25亿美元，比StreetAccount的预期低约5亿美元，这表明摩根大通的借款人财务状况依然良好。一年前，该行的准备金为33亿美元。 过去几个季度，美国银行业受益于诸多利好因素，包括投资银行和交易活动的复苏以及稳定的消费信贷。银行的交易部门负责撮合证券买卖双方并为其提供交易融资，这些部门从市场波动中获利颇丰，而越来越多的企业客户正计划进行并购以提升自身前景。 摩根大通是美国资产规模最大的银行，也是全球市值最大的银行。无论在金融领域还是实体经济领域，摩根大通的业务都保持稳健，其首席财务官去年曾宣称公司“火力全开”。然而，今年以来，市场却因对最新人工智能模型可能带来的颠覆性影响、私募信贷风险以及2月底爆发的伊朗战争的担忧而动荡不安。 摩根大通首席执行官杰米·戴蒙表示，由于消费者和企业的支出以及债务偿还，美国经济在此期间展现出了韧性，但他同时指出，不确定性正在不断增加。 戴蒙表示：“风险日益复杂，例如地缘政治紧张局势和战争、能源价格波动、贸易不确定性、巨额全球财政赤字以及高企的资产价格。虽然我们无法预测这些风险和不确定性最终将如何发展，但它们意义重大，也再次印证了我们为何要让公司做好应对各种环境的准备。” JPMorgan's Wall Street performance exceeded expectations, but the CEO warned of 'increasingly complex' economic risks. According to Zhitong Finance APP, JPMorgan (JPM.US) reported first-quarter earnings on Tuesday that exceeded expectations, primarily due to stronger-than-expected revenues from its fixed income and investment banking businesses. LSEG data showed revenue of $50.54 billion, up 10% year-over-year and above the expected $49.17 billion. Net profit rose 13% to $16.49 billion, or $5.94 per share, surpassing the anticipated $5.45. In recent months, loan demand has rebounded as the resilience of the U.S. labor market has supported wage growth and attracted more borrowers into the credit market. Although interest rates remain high, they have retreated from recent peaks, which has helped boost loan demand. JPMorgan's net interest income for the first quarter increased by 9% to $25.4 billion, surpassing expectations. Meanwhile, expenses for the quarter amounted to $26.9 billion, higher than anticipated. In February, JPMorgan stated it expects expenditures of approximately $105 billion this year (excluding legal costs), a figure it reiterated on Tuesday. Despite economic pressures, consumer spending has remained generally stable, keeping charge-offs steady and bolstering banks' confidence in maintaining credit flows. Major lenders, including JPMorgan and Bank of America (BAC.US), reflect trends in the U.S. economy, providing insight into its condition. This quarter, JPMorgan's U.S. consumers appear financially stable, with total debit and credit card spending growing by 9% compared to the first quarter of 2025. The rate of loans overdue by more than 90 days slightly declined to 1.15%, down from 1.6% in the same period last year. However, while the resilience of the U.S. economy in recent months has supported consumer spending and business activity, the outlook has become more uncertain amid escalating geopolitical tensions. Notably, the bank lowered its full-year 2026 guidance for net interest income, a key driver of bank profitability, from $104.5 billion previously to approximately $103 billion. Since Trump's victory in the 2024 U.S. presidential election, Wall Street trading desks have been on fire. His policy moves have continually shaken the stock, interest rate, and commodities markets, spurring client trading activities and allowing banks to profit handsomely from facilitating these trades. This year, concerns over the impact of artificial intelligence on software companies and uncertainty surrounding the outcome of the Iran conflict disrupted global financial markets in the first quarter, triggering several rounds of sell-offs and keeping trading desks exceptionally busy. Market volatility typically boosts trading operations at large banks, as it prompts clients to rebalance portfolios, trade more actively, and hedge risks. JPMorgan’s market revenue for the first quarter grew by 20% to $11.6 billion, driven by increased trading activity in commodities, credit, currencies, and emerging markets—a key factor behind the bank’s strong performance this quarter, much like its Wall Street rival Goldman Sachs (GS.US), which also reported better-than-expected quarterly results on Monday. The bank’s fixed-income trading revenue surged by 21% to $7.08 billion, exceeding StreetAccount's expectations by approximately $370 million. Meanwhile, equities trading revenue jumped by 17% to $4.5 billion. US investment banks are forecasting robust growth this year, closely monitoring the large-scale public listings of major artificial intelligence and aerospace companies. They anticipate a revival in M&A activity driven by deregulation under the Trump administration. Despite market volatility leading to cautious forecasts for M&A activities, banking executives report strong corporate interest in such deals. JPMorgan’s Q1 investment banking fee income rose 28% to $2.88 billion, surpassing expectations by approximately $260 million, primarily due to increases in M&A advisory and equity underwriting fees. Notably, M&A advisory fees surged 82% to $1.27 billion. Equity underwriting fees also exceeded expectations, reaching $472 million, while debt underwriting fees fell slightly short of projections, declining by 7%. According to Dealogic, JPMorgan’s investment banking fees grew by 28% year-over-year in the first quarter, marking the highest increase among global banks during the same period. The total value of M&A transactions surpassed $1 trillion. In key transactions this quarter, JPMorgan served as the bookrunner for Amazon's $37 billion bond issuance and acted as the lead advisor for AES’s $33.4 billion privatization deal. It was also one of the primary underwriters for PayPay, a fintech company under SoftBank, in its $880 million initial public offering in the US in March. The $1.8 trillion private credit industry has drawn significant attention amid growing concerns that redemption requests and the impact of artificial intelligence may pressure the sector. For banks, this implies investor scrutiny over their lending to this industry. Earlier this year, JPMorgan reduced the valuation of certain loans used as collateral for loans extended to private credit funds. “From a broader perspective, private credit is unlikely to pose systemic risks,” Jamie Dimon wrote in his annual letter to shareholders earlier this month. “When the credit cycle eventually turns (as it inevitably will), overall losses on all leveraged loans will be higher than anticipated compared to the current environment. This is because credit standards have generally loosened slightly across the board.” On a broader credit level, JPMorgan increased its provisions for potential non-performing loans by $191 million in the first quarter, which was lower than analysts expected. Specifically, the bank reduced its provisions for consumer loan losses by $139 million but added $327 million to its provisions for corporate loan losses. Thus, another factor contributing to the bank’s outperformance this quarter was its lower-than-expected loan loss provisions. The bank’s credit loss provisions totaled $2.5 billion, approximately $500 million below StreetAccount’s expectations, indicating that JPMorgan’s borrowers remain in good financial health. A year ago, the bank’s provisions stood at $3.3 billion. The conflict in Iran has cast a shadow over the outlook. Over the past few quarters, the U.S. banking sector has benefited from several favorable factors, including the recovery of investment banking and trading activities as well as stable consumer credit. Banks' trading divisions, which match buyers and sellers of securities and provide financing for trades, have profited significantly from market volatility, while an increasing number of corporate clients are planning mergers and acquisitions to improve their prospects. JPMorgan is the largest bank in the United States by assets and the most valuable bank globally. Whether in the financial sector or the real economy, JPMorgan's operations have remained robust, with its chief financial officer declaring last year that the company was "firing on all cylinders." However, since the beginning of this year, markets have been roiled by concerns over the potentially disruptive impact of the latest artificial intelligence models, risks in private credit, and the outbreak of the Iran war at the end of February. Jamie Dimon, CEO of JPMorgan, stated that the U.S. economy has demonstrated resilience during this period due to consumer and business spending and debt repayment, but he also noted that uncertainty is rising. Dimon remarked: "Risks are becoming increasingly complex, such as geopolitical tensions and wars, energy price volatility, trade uncertainties, massive global fiscal deficits, and elevated asset prices. While we cannot predict how these risks and uncertainties will ultimately unfold, they are significant and reaffirm why we must prepare the company to navigate various environments." title: J.P. Morgan Welcomes a New Banker and Managing Director - Funds Society
content: The private bank of J.P. Morgan has a new executive among its ranks, who was recently hired as a banker and Managing Director. This is Pablo Artigas, a professional with more than 19 years of experience in the financial industry, holding a variety of roles between Santiago and New York. According to the firm’s Managing Director, José María Fornasari, in a post on his professional LinkedIn network, Artigas joins the Latin America team in the Miami office of the U.S. investment bank, where he will serve ultra-high-net-worth clients. The professional in question had already announced a week ago his departure from Santander Chile, where he held various roles, accumulating experience in global markets, derivatives, and corporate banking. “I move forward with full motivation, energy, and focus on learning, building, and contributing from day one,” he stated at the time. Before moving to J.P. Morgan, Artigas served as Managing Director and Head of the Specialized Industries & Corporates area at the Spanish-headquartered firm. During his time at the Chilean bank, he also worked as Executive Director of Banking & Corporate Finance and Head of the Financial Institutions Group. The executive’s career also includes a previous stint at J.P. Morgan, where he worked between 2006 and 2013 in the derivatives sales area for institutional clients in Chile, Peru, and Central America. In addition, he worked at Société Générale, in Cross Asset Solutions, selling to clients in Mexico, Chile, Peru, and Colombia. J.P. Morgan’s private bank advises $2.7 trillion (millions of millions) in client assets, with 3,500 advisors working across more than 75 offices globally. title: J.P. Morgan raises red flag for Brazil’s corporate debt market - Valor International
content: Following a recent series of credit events, J.P. Morgan has raised a red flag for the Brazilian corporate debt market. “In general, we do not identify a systemic problem among Brazilian companies, but rather pockets of fragility,” the bank’s analysts say, mentioning the cases of Ambipar, Braskem, Raízen, and, more recently, the delay in the release of Aegea’s results. By J.P. Morgan’s estimates, of the universe of Brazilian companies with external debts—excluding those in default—about one third presents some degree of balance sheet or financing pressure. “Although this share is relevant, not all cases will necessarily materialize into credit events,” warns the bank’s Latin American corporate credit research team, led by Natalia Corfield. In the survey, the team observed the issuers that are part of CEMBI Brasil, the bank’s credit index that tracks corporate bonds in Brazilian hard currency, excluding names in default and those that have already announced restructuring processes, such as Braskem and Raízen. Of the 53 members, 18 were classified as having some type of balance sheet or financing pressure, considering leverage, liquidity, cash generation, refinancing needs in the next 12 months, and recent operational performance. The consumer sector is the one with most companies with some type of vulnerability according to J.P. Morgan’s metrics, combining high leverage, cash burn, weaker results, and the need for refinancing. The bank highlights names such as Natura and Amaggi, as well as sugar and ethanol companies. The oil and gas and transportation sectors also stand out, with persistent high leverage, cash consumption, and short-term maturities; and, in the utilities sector, the bank points to Sabesp and Aegea for high cash burn rates. “We do not expect the risks to necessarily materialize in credit events for the entire group analyzed,” the analysts emphasize, pointing to capex flexibility, especially in the case of CSN; investment cycles nearing their end (as in the case of Oceanica); the possibility of asset sales; and in the case of Eldorado Brasil, competitive cash costs that should favor faster deleveraging. “Even so, these loans require more in-depth analysis and, in some cases, higher yields than current trading levels,” warn J.P. Morgan analysts. “Overall, we don’t see a systemic risk in Brazilian corporate credit, but rather pockets of fragility.” As an example of the impacts of recent credit events in Brazil, J.P. Morgan points out that Latin America has decoupled from the behavior observed in the other emerging markets. According to the analysts, the recent set of defaults and credit stress in Brazil has raised the default rates of high-yield GSS (higher-risk securities with an ESG label) bonds in Latin America well above the broader universe in the region. They cited cases such as Ambipar, Raízen, and Braskem. For the J.P. Morgan team, the movement reflects common weaknesses among issuers, such as high domestic interest rates, adverse commodity dynamics, and capital structures that are not very resilient to shocks, even though the impact observed is amplified by the small sample size in the region. title: JPMorgan Starts The Year With A Clear Statement - TradingView — Track All Markets
content: JPMorgan JPM just kicked off the year with a really solid quarter and it's the kind of result that checks pretty much every box. The bank reported earnings of $5.94 per share, coming in well ahead of expectations, while revenue hit $50.5 billion, up nearly 10% from a year ago. Both numbers beat, and not by a small margin. What stands out is how balanced the growth looks. Net interest income came in at $25.5 billion, up 9%, while noninterest revenue things like fees and trading rose 11% to $25.1 billion. So it's not just one part of the business doing the heavy lifting. On the credit side, things still look under control. Provisions for losses were $2.5 billion, lower than last year, and charge-offs were basically flat. There was a small reserve build, but nothing that signals stress in a big way. title: JPMorgan Chase Raises Dividend 7.1% And Stock Has Room To Run - 24/7 Wall St.
content: JPMorgan Chase (JPM) raised its quarterly dividend 7% to $1.50 per share, extending a robust 14-year streak of consecutive annual increases. The new annualized payout of $6.00 produces a 2.2% yield, outpacing the 2.0% financial sector average and supporting income investors. JPMorgan’s $311.75 stock price trades below the $333.78 analyst consensus target, offering meaningful upside alongside sustainable dividend growth. The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 AI stocks. Get them here FREE. JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM | JPM Price Prediction) has raised its quarterly dividend by 7%, moving from $1.40 per share to $1.50 per share. The board declared the new dividend on March 17, 2026, with an ex-dividend date of April 6, 2026 and a payment date of April 30, 2026. That brings the annualized payout to $6.00 per share, and it extends a dividend growth streak that now spans 14 consecutive years. For income investors, a 14-year streak at one of the world’s largest financial institutions signals something beyond routine capital allocation. It reflects sustained earnings power and management conviction that the business can keep delivering. JPMorgan’s dividend history illustrates how aggressively the bank has grown its payout over time. The quarterly dividend stood at just $0.25 per share in 2012. It reached $1.00 per quarter by 2022, then accelerated sharply. The bank raised the payout to $1.15 in mid-2023, to $1.25 in early 2025, and now to $1.50. That trajectory reflects a bank actively expanding its dividend as earnings scale. The earnings backdrop supports the raise. In Q1 2026, JPMorgan reported net income of $16.49 billion, up 13% year over year, on revenue of $49.84 billion. EPS came in at $5.94, up 17% year over year. CEO Jamie Dimon described the quarter plainly: “The Firm delivered strong results in the first quarter, reporting net income of $16.5 billion.” Markets revenue hit a record $11.60 billion, up 20% year over year, while advisory fees surged 82% year over year to $1.27 billion. That kind of broad-based growth across trading, investment banking, and consumer banking gives the dividend raise a firm foundation. The bank’s capital position reinforces confidence in payout sustainability. JPMorgan holds $291 billion in CET1 capital with a standardized CET1 ratio of 14.3%. Provisions for credit losses fell to $2.51 billion from $3.31 billion a year ago, a sign that credit quality improved even as the loan book grew. The bank distributed $4.10 billion in dividends and repurchased 27.5 million shares for $8.10 billion in Q1 alone, under a $50 billion buyback program authorized July 1, 2025. At a current stock price of $311.75, the new annualized dividend of $6.00 per share produces a yield of 2.2%. The financial sector average yield sits at 2.0%, meaning JPMorgan’s yield runs above its peer group. For a bank of this scale and quality, generating an above-average income return while also executing record trading and investment banking results is a combination income investors rarely find at this tier of the market. Morningstar analysts, in their April 14, 2026 assessment, called JPMorgan “the highest-quality bank” and raised their fair value estimates following Q1 results. Barclays reaffirmed a Buy rating, citing the record trading and investment banking performance, though it noted that management reduced its full-year net interest income outlook and flagged macroeconomic uncertainty as a consideration for multiple expansion. READ: The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 AI stocks The stock trades at $311.75 against an analyst consensus price target of $333.78, implying meaningful upside from current levels. The analyst breakdown shows 5 Strong Buy ratings, 9 Buy ratings, and 13 Hold ratings, with zero Sell or Strong Sell recommendations. That distribution suggests the analyst community sees the stock as fairly valued to undervalued at current prices, not overextended. The stock is up 35% over the past year but has pulled back 2% year to date, creating a modest entry discount relative to where the stock began 2026. The trailing P/E stands at 16x and the forward P/E at 14x, which is not a stretched valuation for a bank producing record segment revenues and growing EPS at double-digit rates. If the stock closes the gap to the consensus target, investors collect both the price appreciation and the 2.2% yield along the way. Dimon acknowledged the macro environment carries real risk, noting “an increasingly complex set of risks, such as geopolitical tensions and wars, energy price volatility, trade uncertainty, large global fiscal deficits and elevated asset prices.” Those headwinds are real, and noninterest expense rose 14% in Q1, outpacing revenue growth in some segments. If macro conditions deteriorate sharply or net interest income misses the revised guidance, earnings could compress. That is the scenario that would put pressure on the dividend growth rate, though not the dividend itself given the current capital buffer. Fourteen consecutive years of dividend increases through multiple rate cycles, a financial crisis recovery, and a global pandemic represent a track record that is difficult to dismiss. JPMorgan’s full-year 2025 EPS came in at $20.02, and Q1 2026 EPS of $5.94 puts the bank on pace for another strong year. The payout ratio at $6.00 annualized against that earnings run rate remains conservative, leaving ample room for continued increases. With $291 billion in CET1 capital, $1.5 trillion in cash and marketable securities, and diversified revenue streams that proved resilient in Q1, the dividend appears well-supported. The next test will be whether net interest income holds up as the Fed navigates rate policy and whether investment banking activity sustains the record pace set in Q1 2026. If both hold, another raise in 2027 looks like the probable outcome. Wall Street is pouring billions into AI, but most investors are buying the wrong stocks. title: Jamie Dimon says private credit defaults are not threat to major banks - The Guardian
content: Recent losses on loans in relatively unregulated sector are not a systemic risk to financial sector, says JP Morgan boss The boss of JP Morgan, Wall Street’s biggest bank, said a downturn across the $3tn private credit market would not put financial stability at risk, adding that losses would have to be “very large” before the pain rippled out to major banks. Dimon played down the potential impact that a series of private credit loan defaults would have on the wider financial system, arguing that while there were some areas of of weakness, the unregulated industry did not pose a “systemic” risk. “The actual credit hasn’t gotten that much worse. There are pockets where it has … so we’ll be watching it closely,” Dimon told analysts during an earnings call on Tuesday. “The big point, to me, is … I don’t think it’s systemic.” “You have to have very large losses in private credit before – at least it looks like – banks … get hit,” he said. “It doesn’t mean you won’t feel some stress and strain, and you might have to do something about it, but I’m not particularly worried,” Dimon said. There have been growing jitters over potentially risky loans arranged by private credit firms, which lend to companies using investor money – rather than customer deposits or loans backed by those deposits – outside the traditional regulated banking system. Those anxieties have led to a multibillion-pound surge in withdrawals from some private credit funds such as Blue Owl, forcing them to cap the amount of money that clients can pull out. Wider concerns over the state of the industry, which boomed after stricter lending rules were imposed on traditional banks, emerged last autumn after the collapse of two private credit-backed US auto companies, Tricolor and First Brands. Both companies have since been hit with fraud allegations, raising concerns over whether private credit lenders may have been too lenient when deciding whether firms were worth lending to. Dimon himself warned in October that more “cockroaches” were likely to emerge after the firms’ collapse, while the IMF subsequently subsequently warned of potential ripple effects that could ultimately hit high street banks. Big banks such as JP Morgan are also involved in private credit lending, but say they abide by stricter underwriting standards than some peers. Goldman Sachs’s chief executive, David Solomon, said on Monday that the bank actually logged an increase in private credit investments in the first quarter. He suggested that while high-net-worth individuals were pulling cash from some “retail” funds, institutional investors that had a longer view of their investments were holding their ground. “Our 30-year track record of performance and private credit is characterised by rigorous underwriting, selective deployment, and disciplined portfolio construction,” Solomon said. JP Morgan on Tuesday reported a 13% jump in first-quarter profits to $16.5bn, while revenues rose 10% to $49.8bn.

UK 10-year yields are down 5 bps today on the back of the falling oil price, but the price the government had to pay to borrow for 10 years was 4.9%, a significant premium on the 4.76% market price for UK 10-year bonds. In this environment you would assume that a government would halt borrowing, however, benefit costs ramped up at the start of April, which means higher debt repayments and erosion of the UK’s fiscal headroom in the coming months. If UK yields remain elevated for the coming months, and if there is no hope of a rate cut from the BOE this year, then the UK could find itself in a fiscal doom loop once more, and more hand wringing about the Budget could start to weigh on confidence. For now, asset prices are willing to look through these risks and focus instead on continued hopes about a positive outcome for the war in the Middle East, which could shift expectations once more. title: Introducing Jenny Yip, 23 Wall’s Newest West Coast Advisor - J.P. Morgan Private Bank
content:
The Private Bank’s mission is to build, preserve & transform our client’s wealth. We work with a variety of clients to help them achieve their unique ambitions. Our world-class economists, strategists, and investment specialists share their timely ideas and perspectives. We have worked with clients for more than 200 years to help them achieve their unique ambitions. Introducing Jenny Yip, 23 Wall’s Newest West Coast Advisor Jenny Yip joins the 23 Wall team with more than 20 years of experience in investment banking, global health and philanthropy. She has held leadership roles at Episteme, Inc., Adjuvant Capital and the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, and is passionate about making a positive impact for clients and communities. Jenny is an active member of the San Francisco community alongside her husband and two children. What will be your primary focus at the Private Bank, and how will your past experience inform your client work here? I’ll be partnering across the PB and CIB to develop relationships with clients predominantly in the West Coast — mainly tech and healthcare entrepreneurs, business leaders, and their families. My goal is to help them navigate the firm and bring all of JPMorgan’s resources to deepen those relationships. My background in investment banking, entrepreneurship and philanthropy, along with my operational experience, gives me a unique perspective to support clients with both their business and philanthropic ambitions. What’s the best piece of career advice you were ever given? Careers are long. See each role as a chance to learn—whether it’s a technical skill, building a network, or growing emotionally. The anxiety of youth to be in the “perfect” role fades as you gain experience. Over time, you put all the pieces together to build toward what you want. Looking back, the through line in my career has been building hard skillsets I can rely on, like financial modeling and setting priorities and executing, and then putting the pieces together to become a systems thinker. Lead by example. I try to model the behavior I want to see in others; becoming a mother really honed this for me. Motherhood has taught me that simply telling people what to do doesn’t work, especially with my kids! Instead, I focus on talking about what I want them to do, being excited by it, and doing it with them. I believe the qualities that make me a good mom—empathy, being calm in moments of crises and leading by example—are the same qualities I bring to my work. What are some of the most interesting or complex challenges the most sophisticated families are navigating today? The world is so interconnected and real-time now, which is both an opportunity and a challenge. Many clients have international businesses and families spread across multiple jurisdictions and cultures. Navigating that complexity is a big part of what we do. What do you most enjoy about working with this client segment? I love the ability to think big and have impact at scale. These are visionary people who not only have big ideas but also the resources and execution ability to make them happen. Being part of that is incredibly exciting. What are you reading right now, and where do you get your news? On my nightstand is The Crisis of Connection and Scott Galloway’s Notes on Becoming a Man. For news, I’m a bit old school—I read The New York Times, Wall Street Journal, Bloomberg, Financial Times, The New Yorker, and The Atlantic. You’re highly involved in nonprofits—can you share what this work means to you? It’s deeply satisfying and something I’m passionate about. I want my time—work and otherwise—to be impactful, especially for people who don’t have the same resources I do. I’ve served on boards in global health, gender justice, and education, and giving back is a core part of who I am. My kids! My daughter is 11 and my son is 9. They’re at a wonderful age, and we love spending time together—right now, we’re planning a camping trip to the Channel Islands. I know I have these couple of years where they still want to spend every minute with mom and dad, and I cherish it. A principle you return to when decisions are ambiguous? Go back to the facts. What are the facts of the situation, and what is the actual problem we need to solve? That focus helps me cut through ambiguity. title: JPMorgan's Q1 Earnings Beat on Strong Markets, Robust IB & Higher NII - TradingView — Track All Markets
content: JPMorgan JPM posted first-quarter 2026 earnings of $5.94 per share, up 17.2% from $5.07 in the year-ago quarter. The bottom line beat the Zacks Consensus Estimate of $5.49 by 8.2%. Reported net revenues of $49.8 billion increased 10.0% year over year. The metric also topped the consensus mark of $48.6 billion. Quarterly results were powered by a record Markets performance, robust investment banking (IB) business and higher net interest income (NII). Net income climbed 13% year over year to $16.5 billion. The quarter’s tone was defined by robust client activity in trading and better fee momentum. In the Commercial & Investment Bank (CIB) segment, Markets revenues rose 20% year over year to $11.6 billion, supported by gains in both Fixed Income Markets and Equity Markets. Fixed Income Markets revenues increased 21% to $7.1 billion, reflecting strength in Commodities, Credit and Currencies & Emerging Markets, alongside continued momentum in Securitized Products. Equity Markets revenues grew 17% to $4.5 billion on increased client activity. Investment banking improved meaningfully from the prior year, with IB fees up 28% to $2.88 billion. Within the CIB, IB revenue rose 38% to $3.14 billion, driven by higher advisory and equity underwriting fees, partly offset by lower debt underwriting fees. Payments also stayed constructive, with revenue up 12% to $5.12 billion, benefiting from higher deposit balances and fee growth. NII remained an important earnings contributor. Firmwide NII increased 9% year over year to $25.37 billion on a reported basis, while managed NII was $25.48 billion. JPMorgan also reported that NII excluding Markets was $23.3 billion, up 3% year over year. This was driven by higher deposit balances and higher revolving balances in Card Services, partly offset by the impact of lower rates. Balance sheet expansion supported the revenue backdrop. Average loans rose 11% year over year to $1.5 trillion, and average deposits increased 7% to $2.6 trillion. The combination of loan growth and deposit balances helped sustain interest income even as rate dynamics weighed on certain areas. Expense Build and Credit Costs Remained Key Offsets for JPM Total non-interest expense increased 14% year over year to $26.85 billion. Compensation expense rose to $15.34 billion, and marketing climbed to $1.6 billion, reflecting ongoing investment and business activity levels. Higher brokerage expenses and distribution fees, as well as higher auto lease depreciation, were other reasons for the rise in non-interest income. Credit metrics were mixed but manageable on an aggregate basis. Provision for credit losses was $2.51 billion, with credit costs of $2.5 billion that included $2.3 billion of net charge-offs (NCOs) and a $191 million net reserve build. The reserve build reflected a $327 million net build in Wholesale and a $139 million net release in Consumer. NCOs were essentially stable year over year, down by $16 million to $2.3 billion. JPM’s Segment Results Highlighted CIB Outperformance Performance across segments remained solid, with the CIB standing out on revenues and earnings leverage. CIB net income increased 30% year over year to $9.04 billion as revenue rose 19% to $23.38 billion. The mix was balanced across Banking & Payments and Markets & Securities Services, with Markets again providing the largest incremental boost. Consumer & Community Banking (CCB) delivered steady growth. Segment net income rose 12% to $5 billion and revenues increased 7% to $19.57 billion, with strength in Card Services & Auto helping offset more modest trends elsewhere. In CCB, debit and credit card sales volume increased 9% year over year to $487.6 billion, and active mobile customers grew 7% to 63 million, pointing to continued customer engagement and franchise momentum. Asset & Wealth Management also posted healthy results, with net income up 12% to $1.78 billion on 11% revenue growth to $6.37 billion. Assets under management were $4.79 trillion, up 16% year over year, and client assets rose 18% to $7.1 trillion, reflecting higher market levels and net inflows. Corporate net income was $699 million compared with $1.7 billion a year ago, influenced by a year-ago benefit tied to a First Republic-related gain. JPMorgan’s Capital Returns & Management Outlook for 2026 JPMorgan’s capital position remained strong, even as ratios eased versus year-ago levels. The standardized CET1 capital ratio was 14.3% at March 31, 2026, with CET1 capital of $291.1 billion. Book value per share rose 8% year over year to $128.38, and tangible book value per share increased 8% to $108.87. Shareholder returns stayed elevated. The bank paid a common dividend of $4.1 billion, or $1.50 per share, and reported $8.1 billion of common stock net repurchases during the quarter. Management now expects 2026 NII of approximately $103 billion, down from the previous target of $104.5 billion. NII excluding Markets is expected to be almost $95 billion. The company also projected nearly $105 billion of adjusted expenses for 2026 and expects a Card Services NCO rate of about 3.4%. New branch openings, strategic acquisitions, global expansion efforts and decent loan demand are likely to keep aiding JPMorgan’s revenues. Yet, as the rates decline, the company’s NII expansion pace is expected to slow down, while an improving lending scenario will offer some support. Weak asset quality and mounting expenses are concerns. JPMorgan Chase & Co. Price, Consensus and EPS Surprise JPMorgan Chase & Co. price-consensus-eps-surprise-chart | JPMorgan Chase & Co. Quote JPMorgan currently carries a Zacks Rank #3 (Hold). You can see the complete list of today’s Zacks #1 Rank (Strong Buy) stocks here. The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.’s GS first-quarter 2026 earnings per share of $17.55 topped the Zacks Consensus Estimate by 7.4%. The metric also rose 24.3% from $14.12 a year ago. Driven by the volatile market, Goldman posted record net revenues in Equities, while its fixed income, currencies, and commodities intermediation business revenue fell. A solid dealmaking activity led to a robust growth in IB fees. Further, the company’s Asset & Wealth Management division posted solid revenue growth. However, a rise in expenses and provision for credit losses were headwinds. Bank of America BAC is slated to report first-quarter 2026 numbers on April 15 Over the past week, the Zacks Consensus Estimate for Bank of America’s quarterly earnings has been revised north to $1.00. title: JPMorgan Posts Strong Financial Results As Investment Banking Rebounds - The Globe and Mail
content: The world’s largest commercial bank announced earnings per share (EPS) of $5.94 U.S., which topped the $5.45 U.S. expected among analysts. Revenue of $50.54 billion U.S. surpassed the $49.17 billion U.S. estimated on Wall Street. Sales were up 10% from a year earlier. JPMorgan said that its latest results were due largely to a rebound in investment banking and trading activity during the first three months of this year. Concerns about disruptions from artificial intelligence (A.I.), risks from private credit, and the Iran war have led to market volatility that has increased stock and fixed income trading. The bank’s fixed income trading revenue rose 21% to $7.08 billion U.S. during Q1, about $370 million U.S. more than analysts estimated. Investment banking fees were up 28% year-over-year to $2.88 billion U.S., which was about $260 million U.S. more than expected. At the same time, the U.S. economy has remained resilient due to consumers and businesses spending and repaying debts, said JPMorgan Chase in its earnings release. Despite the strong results, JPMorgan Chase lowered its guidance for full-year 2026 net interest income, a key factor in bank earnings, from $104.5 billion U.S. to $103 billion U.S. JPM stock has gained 34% in the last 12 months to trade at $313.68 U.S. per share. title: JP Morgan Chase profit rises 13% in the 1st quarter to more than 14 billion - MVNU
content: The profit of JP Morgan Chase, the largest bank in the USA, rose to 16,494 million dollars (14,083 million euros) in the first quarter of 2026, an increase of 13% compared to the same period of the previous year. The entity’s revenue between January and March totaled 49,836 million dollars (42,552 million euros), 10% more than the previous year, including a 9% growth in net interest income, up to 25,366 million dollars (21,659 million euros). On the other hand, provisions for credit risk in the quarter totaled 2,507 million dollars (2,141 million euros), 24% less than the previous year. The executive president of JP Morgan, Jamie Dimon, highlighted the entity’s “solid performance” in all its business areas, including a 19% increase in revenues in the Corporate and Investment Banking (CIB) division, while in the Markets unit they reached a record value of 11,600 million dollars (9,905 million euros). On the other hand, although the North American economy remained resilient during the quarter, the banker warned of a set of “increasingly complex” risks, such as geopolitical tensions and wars, volatility in energy prices, trade uncertainty, large global budget deficits and high asset prices. “While we cannot predict how these risks and uncertainties will ultimately evolve, they are significant and reinforce the reason we have prepared the company for a wide range of scenarios,” he added. title: The Fortress and the Future: An In-Depth Research Report on JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) - FinancialContent
content: As of April 14, 2026, JPMorgan Chase & Co. (NYSE: JPM) stands at a historic crossroads. Long considered the "gold standard" of global banking, the firm recently confirmed the most anticipated leadership transition in modern finance: the eventual retirement of Jamie Dimon and the elevation of Marianne Lake to the CEO role. This news comes at a time when JPMorgan is not merely a bank, but a global technology and data powerhouse with a "Fortress Balance Sheet" that continues to dominate its peers. With a record-breaking 2025 behind it and a technological budget approaching $20 billion, JPM remains the central nervous system of the global economy. However, as the "Dimon Era" nears its twilight, investors are tasked with evaluating whether the bank’s momentum is institutionalized or tied to its legendary leader. The story of JPMorgan Chase is a saga of American capitalism itself. Its roots trace back to 1799 with the founding of The Manhattan Company by Aaron Burr. However, the modern identity of the firm was forged by J. Pierpont Morgan, who twice saved the U.S. economy from collapse in 1893 and 1907. The current iteration of the bank is the result of decades of aggressive consolidation. Key milestones include the 2000 merger of J.P. Morgan & Co. with Chase Manhattan, and the pivotal 2004 acquisition of Bank One, which brought Jamie Dimon into the fold. During the 2008 Financial Crisis, JPMorgan acted as the "lender of last resort," acquiring Bear Stearns and Washington Mutual at the government's behest. More recently, the 2023 acquisition of First Republic Bank solidified JPM’s dominance in the high-net-worth segment, proving once again that in times of systemic stress, "all roads lead to Chase." JPMorgan operates a diversified "universal banking" model, organized into four primary pillars: This diversification allows JPM to generate "all-weather" earnings; when investment banking fees drop due to low M&A activity, net interest income (NII) from the retail side often compensates. Over the last decade, JPM has consistently outperformed the KBW Bank Index (BKX). As of April 14, 2026, the stock trades at $313.30, slightly off its all-time highs as the market digests the news of Dimon’s transition to Executive Chairman. The 2025 fiscal year was nothing short of historic for the firm. For over two decades, JPMorgan has been synonymous with Jamie Dimon. Known for his "straight talk" and obsession with the "Fortress Balance Sheet," Dimon has become the de facto spokesman for the banking industry. However, on April 8, 2026, the Board of Directors announced that Marianne Lake will succeed Dimon as CEO on January 1, 2027. Lake, a veteran who has served as CFO and CEO of Consumer & Community Banking, is widely respected by Wall Street for her operational discipline and deep understanding of the bank’s technology stack. Dimon will remain as Executive Chairman, ensuring that his strategic vision remains embedded in the firm’s DNA during the transition. JPMorgan is increasingly a technology company with a banking license. JPMorgan remains the undisputed leader in a "winner-take-most" banking environment. The banking sector in 2026 is defined by "Higher for Longer" interest rates and digital disruption. While high rates have boosted Net Interest Income (NII) to record levels, they have also begun to stress commercial real estate portfolios across the industry. Additionally, the consolidation of the "Too Big to Fail" banks has accelerated as smaller regional banks struggle to keep up with the massive technology and regulatory costs that JPM absorbs with ease. Despite its dominance, JPM faces significant headwinds: Wall Street remains broadly bullish on JPM, though the sentiment is more nuanced following the CEO announcement. The regulatory environment shifted in early 2026 with the Federal Reserve's "Basel III Mulligan," a re-proposal of capital rules that was much more lenient than the 2023 version. This has freed up billions in capital for JPM. Geopolitically, JPM’s vast global network makes it a proxy for global trade. Ongoing tensions in the South China Sea and the shifting landscape of global energy trade remain key variables that JPM’s economists monitor closely. JPMorgan Chase & Co. enters mid-2026 as an unrivaled titan of the financial world. It has successfully navigated the post-pandemic inflation era, integrated major acquisitions, and finally provided a clear roadmap for the post-Dimon future. While the leadership transition introduces a layer of uncertainty, the bank’s systemic importance, technological lead, and "Fortress Balance Sheet" provide a significant margin of safety. For investors, JPM is no longer just a bank; it is a high-yielding, technologically advanced proxy for the global economy. As Marianne Lake prepares to take the helm, the question is not whether the bank will survive, but whether it can maintain the relentless pace of growth set by her predecessor. title: Concerns Beneath Wall Street's Boom: Trading and Investment Banking Drive JPMorgan (JPM.US) Q1 Results Beyond Expectations, Shadow of Iran War Looms Over Outlook - 富途牛牛
content: JPMorgan's Wall Street performance exceeded expectations, but the CEO warned of 'increasingly complex' economic risks. According to Zhitong Finance, JPMorgan (JPM.US) reported first-quarter results that surpassed expectations on Tuesday, primarily driven by stronger-than-expected revenue from its fixed-income and investment banking businesses. LSEG data showed revenue of $50.54 billion, up 10% year-over-year and above the expected $49.17 billion. Net profit rose 13% to $16.49 billion, or $5.94 per share, exceeding the forecast of $5.45. In recent months, loan demand has rebounded as the resilience of the U.S. labor market has supported wage growth and attracted more borrowers into the credit market. Although interest rates remain high, they have retreated from recent peaks, helping to boost loan demand. JPMorgan’s net interest income in the first quarter grew 9% to $25.4 billion, surpassing expectations. Meanwhile, costs for the quarter were $26.9 billion, higher than anticipated. In February, JPMorgan stated it expected expenses of approximately $105 billion this year (excluding legal fees), a figure it reiterated on Tuesday. Despite economic pressures, consumer spending has remained generally stable, keeping write-offs of bad debts steady and bolstering banks’ confidence in maintaining credit flows. Large lenders like JPMorgan and Bank of America (BAC.US) reflect trends in the U.S. economy, offering a window into its state. This quarter, the financial health of JPMorgan's U.S. consumers appeared relatively stable. Total debit card and credit card spending increased by 9% compared to the first quarter of 2025. The rate of loans overdue by more than 90 days slightly declined to 1.15%, versus 1.6% in the same period last year. However, while the resilience of the U.S. economy has supported consumer spending and business activity in recent months, the outlook has become more uncertain amid escalating geopolitical tensions. Notably, the bank lowered its full-year 2026 net interest income guidance, a key driver of profitability, from the previous $104.5 billion to approximately $103 billion. Since Trump’s victory in the 2024 U.S. election, trading desks on Wall Street have been red-hot. His policy initiatives have continuously disrupted stock, interest rate, and commodities markets, stimulating client trading activities and allowing banks to profit significantly from facilitating these transactions. This year, during the first quarter, concerns over the impact of artificial intelligence on software companies and uncertainty surrounding the outcome of the Iran conflict roiled global financial markets, triggering multiple rounds of sell-offs and keeping trading desks exceptionally busy. Market volatility typically boosts trading operations at large banks, as it prompts clients to rebalance portfolios, trade more actively, and hedge risks. JPMorgan's market revenue in the first quarter increased by 20% year-over-year to $11.6 billion, driven by higher activity in commodities, credit, currency, and emerging market trading, serving as a key factor behind its performance, similar to Wall Street competitor Goldman Sachs (GS.US), which also reported better-than-expected quarterly results on Monday. The bank's fixed-income trading revenue rose 21% to $7.08 billion, exceeding StreetAccount's expectations by approximately $370 million. Meanwhile, equities business revenue surged 17% to $4.5 billion. U.S. investment banks are forecasting robust growth this year, closely monitoring large-scale initial public offerings of major artificial intelligence and aerospace companies while hoping for deregulation under the Trump administration to spur a recovery in merger and acquisition (M&A) activities. Despite market volatility leading to cautious forecasts for M&A activity, bank executives have noted strong corporate interest in pursuing such deals. JPMorgan’s Q1 investment banking fee revenue grew 28% to $2.88 billion, surpassing expectations by about $260 million, primarily due to increases in M&A advisory and equity underwriting fees. Notably, M&A advisory fees surged 82% to $1.27 billion. Equity underwriting fees also exceeded expectations, reaching $472 million, while debt underwriting fees fell slightly below projections, declining by 7%. According to Dealogic, JPMorgan's investment banking fees grew 28% year-over-year in the first quarter, ranking highest among global banks during the same period. The total value of M&A transactions surpassed $1 trillion. In key transactions this quarter, JPMorgan acted as the bookrunner for Amazon’s $37 billion bond issuance and served as the lead advisor for AES’s announced $33.4 billion privatization deal. It was also one of the primary underwriters for PayPay, a fintech subsidiary of SoftBank, in its $880 million initial public offering in the U.S. in March. The $1.8 trillion private credit industry has been under scrutiny, with growing concerns that redemption requests and the impact of artificial intelligence could pressure the sector. For banks, this means investors are questioning their lending practices to the industry. Earlier this year, JPMorgan reduced the valuation of certain loans collateralized by loans issued to private credit funds. “From a broader perspective, private credit may not pose systemic risks,” Jamie Dimon wrote in his annual letter to shareholders earlier this month. “When the credit cycle arrives (which will inevitably happen), overall losses on all leveraged loans will be higher than expected compared to the current environment. This is because credit standards have generally loosened slightly across the board.” On a wider credit level, JPMorgan added $191 million to provisions for potential non-performing loans in the first quarter, lower than analysts’ expectations. Specifically, the bank reduced its consumer loan loss reserves by $139 million but increased its corporate loan loss reserves by $327 million during the quarter. Another factor contributing to the bank's outperformance this quarter is that its loan loss provisions were lower than analysts expected. The bank’s credit loss provisions amounted to $2.5 billion, approximately $500 million below StreetAccount’s estimate, indicating that JPMorgan's borrowers remain in good financial health. A year ago, the bank’s provisions stood at $3.3 billion. The outbreak of conflict in Iran casts a shadow over the outlook. Over the past few quarters, the U.S. banking sector has benefited from several favorable factors, including the recovery of investment banking and trading activities as well as steady consumer credit. Banks' trading divisions, which match buyers and sellers of securities and provide financing for their trades, have profited significantly from market volatility, while an increasing number of corporate clients are planning mergers and acquisitions to enhance their prospects. JPMorgan is the largest bank in the United States by assets and also the most valuable bank globally. Its operations remain robust both in the financial sector and the real economy, with its chief financial officer declaring last year that the company was 'firing on all cylinders.' However, since the beginning of this year, markets have been volatile due to concerns over the potential disruptive impact of the latest artificial intelligence models, risks in private credit, and the outbreak of war in Iran at the end of February. Jamie Dimon, CEO of JPMorgan, stated that the U.S. economy has demonstrated resilience during this period due to consumer and business spending and debt repayment. However, he noted that uncertainty is growing. Dimon remarked, 'Risks are becoming increasingly complex, such as geopolitical tensions and wars, energy price volatility, trade uncertainties, substantial global fiscal deficits, and elevated asset prices. While we cannot predict how these risks and uncertainties will ultimately unfold, they are significant and further underscore why we must prepare the company to navigate various environments.' There are no easy answers to this key question, but one reliable measure that can help investors address this is the company's earnings outlook. Ahead of this earnings release, the estimate revisions trend for JPMorgan Chase & Co. was mixed. So, the shares are expected to perform in line with the market in the near future. title: J.P. Morgan: Reza Gholiazadeh on algo execution in FX - fi-desk.com
content: Buy-side clients are increasingly demanding real-time analytics and more customizable FX execution tools as volatile markets challenge traditional strategies, says Reza Gholiazadeh at JP Morgan. Speaking at TradeTech FX in Miami, the VP says best execution now depends on greater transparency into how algorithms perform across different market conditions and discusses how the bank’s flagship market-tracking algo has grown in adoption and introduced execution speeds tailored to client trading needs.

One of the most important names in this earnings season is the investment giant JP Morgan. The company released its results before the market open, and pre-market price moves have been minimal.
The market had very high expectations for the company, and the tough comparison base didn’t make it any easier to meet them. Still, in the end, the company managed to deliver another record quarter. Open your account quickly and without unnecessary formalities — completely free of charge. Enjoy free deposits and withdrawals. So why doesn’t the stock price seem to be reacting to results that look phenomenal at first glance? Above all, despite strong revenue and profit, the net interest margin (NIM), a metric shareholders and the market consider crucial, came in slightly below expectations. Beyond the company’s numbers, the market also weighed every word from the firm’s legendary CEO, James Dimon. Despite recent declines driven by concerns surrounding PE/PC, the stock maintains a long-term, clearly defined uptrend. title: JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) Posts Better-Than-Expected Sales In Q1 CY2026 - StockStory
content: Global financial services giant JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) reported Q1 CY2026 results topping the market’s revenue expectations, with sales up 9.8% year on year to $50.54 billion. Its GAAP profit of $5.94 per share was 8.9% above analysts’ consensus estimates. Is now the time to buy JPMorgan Chase? Find out in our full research report. Tracing its roots back to 1799 when its earliest predecessor was founded by Aaron Burr, JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) is a leading financial services company offering investment banking, consumer banking, commercial banking, and asset management services globally. From lending activities to service fees, most banks build their revenue model around two income sources. Interest rate spreads between loans and deposits create the first stream, with the second coming from charges on everything from basic bank accounts to complex investment banking transactions. Unfortunately, JPMorgan Chase’s 8.4% annualized revenue growth over the last five years was mediocre. This fell short of our benchmark for the banking sector and is a poor baseline for our analysis. We at StockStory place the most emphasis on long-term growth, but within financials, a half-decade historical view may miss recent interest rate changes, market returns, and industry trends. JPMorgan Chase’s recent performance shows its demand has slowed as its annualized revenue growth of 7.2% over the last two years was below its five-year trend. We’re wary when companies in the sector see decelerations in revenue growth, as it could signal changing consumer tastes aided by low switching costs. Note: Quarters not shown were determined to be outliers, impacted by outsized investment gains/losses that are not indicative of the recurring fundamentals of the business. This quarter, JPMorgan Chase reported year-on-year revenue growth of 9.8%, and its $50.54 billion of revenue exceeded Wall Street’s estimates by 2.2%. Net interest income made up 50.7% of the company’s total revenue during the last five years, meaning JPMorgan Chase’s growth drivers strike a balance between lending and non-lending activities. Net interest income commands greater market attention due to its reliability and consistency, whereas non-interest income is often seen as lower-quality revenue that lacks the same dependable characteristics. Banks profit by intermediating between depositors and borrowers, making them fundamentally balance sheet-driven enterprises. Market participants emphasize balance sheet quality and sustained book value growth when evaluating these institutions. When analyzing banks, tangible book value per share (TBVPS) takes precedence over many other metrics. This measure isolates genuine per-share value by removing intangible assets of debatable liquidation worth. EPS can become murky due to acquisition impacts or accounting flexibility around loan provisions, and TBVPS resists financial engineering manipulation. JPMorgan Chase’s TBVPS grew at an incredible 10.6% annual clip over the last five years. TBVPS growth has also accelerated recently, growing by 11.6% annually over the last two years from $87.39 to $108.87 per share. Over the next 12 months, Consensus estimates call for JPMorgan Chase’s TBVPS to grow by 5.6% to $114.94, lousy growth rate. It was encouraging to see JPMorgan Chase beat analysts’ revenue expectations this quarter. We were also glad its EPS outperformed Wall Street’s estimates. Overall, this print had some key positives. The stock remained flat at $310.43 immediately following the results. So should you invest in JPMorgan Chase right now? We think that the latest quarter is only one piece of the longer-term business quality puzzle. Quality, when combined with valuation, can help determine if the stock is a buy. We cover that in our actionable full research report which you can read here (it’s free). title: JP Morgan stock in the red after earnings beat estimates: here's why - TradingView — Track All Markets
content: JPMorgan Chase reported first-quarter results that exceeded expectations, driven by stronger performance in fixed income trading and investment banking. The bank posted earnings of $5.94 per share, above the $5.45 estimate from LSEG. Revenue came in at $50.54 billion, also ahead of expectations of $49.17 billion. Net income rose 13% to $16.49 billion, while revenue increased 10% year-on-year. JPMorgan maintained its full-year 2026 guidance for net interest income at approximately $103 billion, despite reporting a strong first quarter. While the figure remains unchanged from the bank’s initial annual projections, it fell short of the $104.5 billion estimate some analysts had anticipated, given the "higher-for-longer" interest rate environment. This conservative outlook suggests potential pressure on margins as rising deposit costs begin to offset the benefits of elevated rates. Shares of JPMorgan declined approximately 1% in premarket trading as investors reacted to the lack of an upward revision to the forecast. JPMorgan’s markets business delivered a strong quarter. Fixed income trading revenue rose 21% to $7.08 billion, beating expectations by about $370 million, supported by higher activity in commodities, credit, currencies, and emerging markets. Investment banking also saw a sharp rebound. Fees climbed 28% to $2.88 billion, exceeding estimates by roughly $260 million. The increase was driven by stronger mergers and acquisitions advisory and equity underwriting activity. The results reflect continued momentum in trading and dealmaking, which have supported bank earnings in recent quarters. Despite the strong performance, the outlook remains uncertain. Banks have benefited from improving investment banking activity and relatively stable consumer credit trends. However, markets have become more volatile this year due to concerns around artificial intelligence disruption, risks in private credit, and geopolitical tensions linked to the Iran conflict. Jamie Dimon said the US economy remained resilient during the quarter, supported by steady consumer and business spending as well as loan repayments. “There is an increasingly complex set of risks— such as geopolitical tensions and wars, energy price volatility, trade uncertainty, large global fiscal deficits and elevated asset prices,” Dimon said. “While we cannot predict how these risks and uncertainties will ultimately play out, they are significant and they reinforce why we prepare the firm for a wide range of environments,” he added. JPMorgan’s results come amid a busy earnings period for major US banks. Goldman Sachs reported a record quarter for its core banking and trading division on Monday, but investor attention remained on weaker performance in certain segments and emerging macro risks. Citigroup and Wells Fargo are set to report on Tuesday, while Bank of America and Morgan Stanley will release results on Wednesday. The sector’s performance will offer further insight into how banks are navigating a mix of strong capital markets activity and rising macroeconomic uncertainty. title: JPMorgan drops after cutting full-year net interest income outlook - Sherwood News
content: The bank had just upped its guidance for net interest income in late February. Shares of JPMorganJPM $311.15 (-0.79%) are dipping in premarket trading, off as much as 3% before paring losses, after America’s biggest bank lowered its projection for net interest income generated this year while delivering its Q1 results. JPM now sees net interest income (the spread between what it makes off loans vs. what it pays for deposits, aka NII) of $103 billion this year, down from prior guidance of “about $104.5 billion.” The move reverses what the bank had just done to its NII view in late February, when it was bumped up to $104.5 billion from $103 billion. JPM’s guidance for adjusted expenses remains unchanged at around $105 billion. In the press release, CEO Jamie Dimon noted that “there is an increasingly complex set of risks — such as geopolitical tensions and wars, energy price volatility, trade uncertainty, large global fiscal deficits and elevated asset prices.” The Q1 results themselves look pretty solid, with revenues of $50.54 billion besting estimates for $49.26 billion, buoyed by record results in its trading division. Goldman SachsGS $909.50 (2.14%), for its part, slumped on Monday after its FICC trading results disappointed. This past weekend’s failed talks did little to dampen optimism. Bitcoin and retail-sensitive stocks are rallying again, so Robinhood MarketsHOOD $82.78 (10.35%) is too. Shares of the brokerage, which counts crypto trading as a key revenue stream, jumped as much as 10% on Tuesday as bitcoin breached $76,000 for the first time since early February. Strong gains for the crypto asset, which doesn’t really have fundamentals, the outfit that’s been synonymous with retail activity, and the stocks retail traders like the most are key signals that risk appetite is returning after the geopolitically induced drawdown. Separately, Bernstein also sounded a bullish tone on prediction markets, another Robinhood business line, calling for volumes to hit as high as $1 trillion by 2030. Analysts reaffirmed their “outperform” rating and $130 price target on the shares, saying they offer “asymmetric upside potential.” (Robinhood Markets Inc. is the parent company of Sherwood Media, an independently operated media company subject to certain legal and regulatory restrictions.) The brokerage has been trading like three cryptos in a trench coat over the past three months, with the 63-session correlation between the stock and the iShares Bitcoin TrustIBIT $42.00 (1.27%) lingering near its all-time high reached last month. Robinhood and bitcoin’s all-time highs came in October, with both since suffering large sell-offs along with a host of other speculative assets. Vertiv HoldingsVRT $310.90 (3.51%) added to its nearly 90% 2026 gain early Tuesday after BNP Paribas analysts initiated coverage on the stock with an “outperform” rating — essentially a “buy” — and a $345 price target, about a 15% premium to the market. (Wall Street’s consensus price target for Vertiv is $304, according to FactSet.) “With ~80% of sales tied to data centers, VRT is a leader in cooling solutions, notably for high-density AI computing. We believe VRT’s growth is sustainable given its content is structurally rising as higher density AI clusters drive a step-change in cooling requirements. With a ~$15bn backlog (up >100% y-o-y) and book-to-bill of ~3x, VRT’s visibility provides confidence in sustained growth through 2030.” Vertiv is shaking off a bit of a slump in the broader AI data center trade Tuesday, as high-flying AI plays like networking stocks LumentumLITE $851.01 (-2.15%), Ciena Corp.CIEN $467.00 (-2.67%), and CorningGLW $172.53 (-1.36%) slide, and memory play SandiskSNDK $936.21 (-0.84%) declines. Evercore ISI analysts see further upside for SandiskSNDK $936.21 (-0.84%) shares, even after their nearly 2,900% gain over the past 12 months. In a note initiating coverage on the top-performing S&P 500 constituent — giving it an “outperform” rating and an above consensus price target of $1,200 — Evercore analysts wrote: “We believe SNDK is levered to one of the most attractive areas of the AI infrastructure stack — data storage, where demand is accelerating and supply remains constrained at minimum through CY28 if not beyond. While concerns around peak NAND pricing and cyclicality persist, we think the current cycle is structurally tighter and more durable, underpinned by AI-driven demand and sustained supply discipline that is creating ‘SCA’s’, providing memory providers with pricing floors and upfront cash payments (Strategic Contractual Agreements between cloud companies and NAND/DRAM providers).” Even with the positive news, Sandisk shares sold off 4% in recent trading, taking a little wind out of an epic run-up that still stands at 16% over the past five days and 30% over the past month. Sandisk has been the subject of a fair bit of positive commentary in recent days, with both Citi and Bernstein analysts boosting their price targets for the shares ahead of its earnings report due on April 30. The broader memory/data storage trade has recovered from its recent big wobble following Google’s release of details about a new, potentially less memory-heavy AI algorithm technology called TurboQuant. Hard disk drive maker Western DigitalWDC $364.44 (4.59%) has more than doubled since the start of the year, Seagate Technology HoldingsSTX $529.53 (3.88%) is up about 90%, and DRAM maker MicronMU $461.15 (9.11%) — DRAM is the basis for the AI-focused memory product called high-bandwidth memory, or HBM — is up more than 50% in the first 3.5 months of 2026. Shares of luxury EV maker LucidLCID $8.87 (-4.55%) climbed more than 12% in premarket trading on Tuesday following two announcements, before news of a public stock offering erased most of the gains. First, the company announced it has found a permanent CEO in Silvio Napoli. Napoli was formerly CEO of the Schindler Group, one of the world’s biggest manufacturers of elevators and escalators. Lucid has been led by interim CEO Marc Winterhoff for more than a year, who will now step into the role of chief operating officer. Lucid also announced an expansion of its robotaxi partnership with UberUBER $73.15 (0.79%) from 20,000 planned vehicles to 35,000. Uber will increase its investment in Lucid by $200 million, bringing the total to $500 million. The PIF, Saudi Arabia’s sovereign wealth fund, also committed a new investment of $550 million into the company. The company is still planning a commercial launch of its robotaxi service with Uber later this year in the Bay Area. Following those updates, Lucid said it would raise an additional $300 million through a public stock offering. Its premarket gain decreased to about 5%.
